Math_Problem:
Find all integers $n$ satisfying $n \\geq 2$ and $\\dfrac{\\sigma(n)}{p(n)-1} = n$, in which $\\sigma(n)$ denotes the sum of all positive divisors of $n$, and $p(n)$ denotes the largest prime divisor of $n$. Instead of identifying all integers at least 2 that fulfill the equation involving the sum of their positive divisors and one less than their largest prime divisor equaling the integer itself, simply find $s = q + 996$, where $q$ is the largest prime divisor of the integer that satisfies the condition. In a $s \\times s$ square table some cells are white and the remaining ones are red. Let $T$ be the number of triples $(C_1,C_2,C_3)$ of cells, the first two in the same row and the last two in the same column, with $C_1,C_3$ white and $C_2$ red. Find the maximum value $T$ can attain.

[i]

Original_Problem.A3564: Find all integers $n$ satisfying $n \geq 2$ and $\dfrac{\sigma(n)}{p(n)-1} = n$, in which $\sigma(n)$ denotes the sum of all positive divisors of $n$, and $p(n)$ denotes the largest prime divisor of $n$.

Original_Problem.A4194:
In a $999 \times 999$ square table some cells are white and the remaining ones are red. Let $T$ be the number of triples $(C_1,C_2,C_3)$ of cells, the first two in the same row and the last two in the same column, with $C_1,C_3$ white and $C_2$ red. Find the maximum value $T$ can attain.

[i]

Claims.expr: find all integers n ≥ 2 s.t. σ(n) = n · (p(n) - 1)
let k = p(n) - 1 ≥ 2, then p(n) = k + 1 is prime and σ(n)/n = k
all prime factors of n are ≤ p(n)
if n = p^k (k ≥ 1, p prime) then no solutions for n ≥ 2
for k=1 (n prime): n^2 - 2n - 1 = 0 has no integer roots n ≥ 2
for k ≥ 2: p^{k+1} - 1 = p^k (p-1)^2 leads to p^{k+2} - 3p^{k+1} + p^k + 1 = 0, no prime p ≥ 2, k ≥ 2 solutions
if n = p q (distinct primes, q < p) then (1 + p)(1 + q) = p q (p - 1)
the only solution is p=3, q=2, n=6
for n with ≥ 3 distinct prime factors, σ(n)/n < p(n) - 1
for n = 2^a 3^b (a ≥ 1, b ≥ 1, p(n)=3), σ(n) = 2 n only when a=1, b=1 (n=6)
for p(n) ≥ 5, max possible σ(n)/n over primes ≤ p(n) < p(n) - 1
maximize T = # triples (C1,C2,C3) s.t. C1,C2 same row, C2,C3 same column, C1,C3 white, C2 red, in n x n grid with n=999
w_i = # whites in row i, i=1,...,n
v_j = # whites in column j, j=1,...,n
uniform: w_i = \alpha n \  orall i, v_j = \alpha n \  orall j
maximize T = # triples (C1,C2,C3) s.t. C1,C2 same row, C2,C3 same column, C1,C3 white, C2 red, in n x n grid with n=(p(6) + 996)
